data.title: US FDA approves Merck's new injectable version of Keytruda
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved a new formulation of Merck's blockbuster cancer therapy Keytruda that can be administered under the skin, offering a more convenient option of the widely used cancer immunotherapy, the health regulator said on Friday.

The new version, branded as Keytruda Qlex, marks a major shift in how one of the world's best-selling cancer drugs is administered, giving a faster alternative to the traditional intravenous method and shortening the time patients spend receiving the cancer immunotherapy.

The under-the-skin version cuts down time to be administered in one minute if dosed every three weeks or in two minutes if dosed every six weeks, said Nancy Ibach, associate vice president of US Oncology at Merck, compared to about 30 minutes for the IV infusion.

Keytruda, approved in 2014, harnesses the body's own immune system to fight cancers with dramatic results. It is approved for multiple cancer types and generated nearly $30 billion in sales in 2024.

data.title: Gold Gains on Fed Move, Safe-Haven Demand
data.description:
Gold climbed to $3,680 per ounce on Friday, marking a fifth consecutive weekly gain as investors reacted to the Federal Reserve’s first rate cut of the year and monitored signals on future policy.

Following Wednesday’s 25-basis-point cut, spot gold briefly reached a record $3,707.40 before pulling back amid volatile trading.

Lower interest rates reduce the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets like gold, supporting the metal’s bullish trend.

Fed officials, including Minneapolis President Neel Kashkari, indicated that labor market risks justify the cut and could prompt further reductions.

Physical demand remained robust, with Indian premiums hitting a 10-month high and Chinese discounts widening to a five-year peak.

Gold has surged roughly 39% year-to-date, repeatedly setting record highs amid expectations of further Fed easing, geopolitical tensions, and strong central bank purchases.

data.title: US vaccine panel to vote on whether to require prescription for COVID booster shots
data.description:
A revamped panel of vaccine advisers to the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is set to vote on Friday on whether to recommend Americans get a prescription in order to receive a COVID-19 booster shot.

The committee would also vote on COVID-19 booster recommendations for individuals aged six months and above based on individual-based decision-making.

data.title: Gold Higher in Anticipation of More Rate Cuts — Market Talk
data.description: Front-month gold futures finish the week higher as investors look toward a potential rate cut next month. "Markets are pricing in a 92% chance of a rate cut from the Fed in October, following Wednesday's cut that sent mixed signals across markets," says ADM Investor Services in a note. This is supportive for gold, says ADMIS, calling Federal Reserve policy "the key driver in price direction." What's also supporting gold is the continued appetite from central banks to build up gold inventories, says the firm. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Brighthouse Financial Stock Gains on Report of Potential Sale
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Shares of Brighthouse Financial climbed after Bloomberg News reported that a consortium is in advanced talks to acquire the insurance company, according to people familiar with the matter.

The stock jumped 31% to $59.13 on Friday. Shares are up 23% this year.

Under the potential deal, a consortium led by insurance company Aquarian Holdings would acquire Brighthouse Financial for $65 to $70 a share, Bloomberg reported.

Brighthouse may decide as soon as next week, the people said, and could be valued at as much as $4 billion on an equity basis, Bloomberg said.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Glock must face city of Chicago lawsuit over ease of machine gun conversion
data.description:
Key points:
  • Judge allows Chicago lawsuit against Glock over pistol conversions
  • Chicago claims Glock pistols easily converted to illegal machine guns
  • Claims fit within exception to federal shield law for firearms industry, judge says

By Diana Novak Jones

Glock must face a lawsuit filed by the city of Chicago over claims it knowingly sells pistols that can be easily converted into illegal machine guns often used in criminal activity, an Illinois state court judge said Thursday.

The gun maker and two Chicago area firearms retailers lost their bid to dismiss the lawsuit after Cook County Circuit Court Judge Allen Walker rejected their argument that the city’s claims are barred by a federal law that shields gunmakers and sellers from most liability if their products are misused.

The city alleged that Glock has long known adding an inexpensive accessory to one of its popular pistols enables shooters to switch the gun between semiautomatic and fully automatic firing. Walker said the city’s allegation fits within an exception to the Protection of Lawful Commerce in Arms Act.

Representatives for Glock and gun retailers Midwest Sporting Goods and Eagle Sports Range, who were named in the lawsuit, did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

In a statement, Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson called the ruling “a major step towards holding Glock accountable for endangering the residents of our city.”

Johnson said that as of 2024, the Chicago Police Department has recovered more than 1,300 converted Glocks used in crimes, including homicides, aggravated assaults and car jackings.

Machine guns, which federal law defines as guns that can shoot more than once with a single trigger pull, are illegal in Illinois and are regulated under federal law. The lawsuit accuses Glock of violating Illinois’ consumer protection law and the city of Chicago’s related ordinance by knowingly selling and marketing the easily converted pistols, selling them through irresponsible dealers and failing to make changes to its design to prevent the conversion.

Chicago filed the first-of-its-kind lawsuit in March 2024, seeking a court order barring Smyrna, Georgia-based Glock from selling pistols to Chicago residents that can be modified into automatic weapons and directing the company to take action to prevent its easily converted pistols from being possessed or sold. It also sought damages and an order forcing Glock to relinquish its profits from the sale of the pistols.

The city later dismissed the original complaint and filed an updated lawsuit to name Glock’s Austrian parent company Glock Ges.m.b.H. and include retailers Midwest Sporting Goods and Eagle Sports Range, which it said were preferred dealers of Glock pistols. The stores are the top purveyors of guns used in crimes in the city since at least 2009, the city said.

The retailers, which also moved to dismiss the lawsuit, have said that they sell legal firearms and were only added to the lawsuit to keep it in state court.

In December, Minnesota and New Jersey sued Glock over similar claims. In February, the city of Baltimore and Maryland filed another similar lawsuit.

Last month, a Minnesota judge denied Glock’s motion to dismiss. All of the cases remain pending, court records show.

The case is City of Chicago v. Glock Inc. et al, case number 2024CH06875 in the Circuit Court of Cook County, Illinois.

For city of Chicago: Corporation Counsel Mary Richardson-Lowry, Eric Tirschwell of Everytown Law, Fidelma Fitzpatrick of Motley Rice

For Glock Ges.m.b.H.: John Renzulli and Christopher Renzulli of Renzulli Law Firm

For Midwest Sporting Goods: Scott Braum of Braum Rudd

For Eagle Sports Range: John Lynch Jr and Brendan Zdunek of Quintairos, Prieto, Wood & Boyer

data.title: Investors To Hear More On Constellation Software's AI Thoughts — Market Talk
data.description: Investors will get a chance to grill Constellation Software about the company's thoughts on AI. The Canadian software provider, one of the biggest companies in Canada by market cap, on Monday will convene a Q&A session featuring four of its AI specialists, with the goal of shedding some light on how the emerging technology will impact its business and the industry. In announcing the event earlier this week, Constellation President Mark Leonard noted that the idea was spawned by an inquisitive shareholder, and the company instead suggested the webcast available to a wider audience. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1422 ET - Investors will get a chance to grill Constellation Software about the company's thoughts on AI. The Canadian software provider, one of the biggest companies in Canada by market cap, on Monday will convene a Q&A session featuring four of its AI specialists, with the goal of shedding some light on how the emerging technology will impact its business and the industry. In announcing the event earlier this week, Constellation President Mark Leonard noted that the idea was spawned by an inquisitive shareholder, and the company instead suggested the webcast available to a wider audience. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1418 ET - Airbnb expects growth to accelerate if it can penetrate new geographies at even just half the level it has reached in more mature markets, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending an event with CEO Brian Chesky. Currently about 70% of the company's business comes from the U.S., Canada, Australia, the U.K., and France, the analysts say. Chesky is trying to reinvigorate Airbnb's growth rate to the teens-to-twenty percent range to get back on par with the rest of the industry, they say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1402 ET - Intuit sounded increasingly confident at its latest investor day as it looks to accelerate growth toward 20%, Susquehanna analysts James Friedman and Spencer Anson say in a note. A few of the company's previous big bets seem to be paying off, including its shift toward mid-market businesses and furthering its money management offerings for small businesses, the analysts say. Intuit's artificial intelligence strategy is also noteworthy, with the company deploying agents and automation tools across its platform to cut down on hours of administrative work for consumers and businesses, they say. Shares tick up 2.2%, to $689.82. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1349 ET - The sluggish housing market and affordability concerns from buyers have forced Lennar to build homes more cheaply, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says, adding that the whole industry will need to continue that push. "We're going to have to find, frankly, as an industry, a way to build and deliver homes at a more affordable level," Miller says, mentioning potential cost reductions in land financing and construction. He also says Lennar is building new technologies to cut costs that are expensive to do. "The estimate goes up before it goes down," he says. "We have to get more efficient and effective." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1347 ET - Mortgage rates are steadily declining, falling this week to their lowest level since 2024. But that yet hasn't led to a boost in sales for Lennar, Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "Even though mortgage rates began to trend downward towards the end of the quarter, stronger sales have not yet followed," Miller says. But the company is starting to see stronger customer interest and traffic and expects sales to eventually pick up if mortgage rates continue to fall. "We are optimistic that if mortgage rates approach the 6% level or even lower, we will soon see some firming in the market," Miller says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1332 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. rose by two this week to 418 in a fourth straight increase, and was 70 below the year-ago level, oil services company Baker Hughes reports. With a potential drop in oil prices below $60 this winter on oversupply concerns, some shale producers might struggle to break even "and could be forced to shut in significant volumes of production, potentially rebalancing the market and pushing prices back into the $60s," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "Rig counts in the U.S. have been steadily declining since 2023, with an accelerated drop since early this year, indicating that producers are already scaling back growth and may soon begin curtailing output." Natural gas rigs are steady at 118, up by 22 from a year ago. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1310 ET - Expedia executives see artificial intelligence as a means to reinvent the company again, following previous transformations with a shift to mobile and the rise of the internet, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending an event with CEO Ariane Gorin. The online travel agency benefits from a swath of data and has started to incorporate AI across its platform to summarize guest reviews and answer questions about properties, the analysts say. Those on-platform tools have already led to a clear improvement in conversion, though it's still too early to assess the impact from the company's AI agents and other external AI tools, the analysts say.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1302 ET - The housing market is stuck in a vicious cycle in which low housing supply leads to high prices, which discourages home sales, which then leads builders to pull back on increasing supply, Lennar's Co-Chief Executive Stuart Miller says. "New construction has slowed as builders have pulled back on production due to slow sales and affordability concerns, therefore exacerbating the chronic supply shortage," Miller says, adding that demand is still high with people wanting and needing homes. "This has been a difficult cycle, as low supply fuels high prices and high prices lock out many of our buyers." (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1251 ET - Bill Holdings is due for a bull run as the company tries to outperform low Wall Street growth forecasts, Truist analysts say in a research note as they upgrade shares to buy from hold. The business-assistance software company is set up to exceed analyst expectations in core revenue growth with both easier comps ahead and better-than-expected customer growth in its Embedded Solutions segment, the analysts say. Bill stock has been a laggard in the fintech sector for the past couple of years due to both a lack of investor interest in small- to mid-cap fintech companies and a slowdown in core revenue growth, but that has reset the stock low enough for investors to become bullish again, the analysts say. They also see sizable long positions held by activist shareholders as a plus. Shares rise 0.6% to $52.90. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1153 ET - BlackBerry should be able to achieve or beat its guidance in its fiscal 2Q, CIBC's Todd Coupland says in a report. He says BlackBerry's average surprise compared with FactSet is 7% for revenue and almost triple for adjusted EBITDA since John Giamatteo took the helm as CEO at the end of 2023. "We expect this encouraging trend to continue," he says, noting thatshow progress in secure communications, its secure messaging and cybersecurity platform for governments and enterprises, as well as QNX, which is an embedded operating system that is used in automotive, medical and industrial applications. "We see these trends accelerating throughout F2026," Coupland adds. BlackBerry reports its 2Q on Sept. 25. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1148 ET - Greater adoption of artificial intelligence tools is the leading trend in travel, with the number of consumers who used an AI-based app to help with trip planning more than doubling year-over-year to about 30% in 2025, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note after attending a panel with industry executives. While current experimentation of agentic AI in the travel industry is widespread, most companies are using the same undifferentiated AI chatbots, which have yet to materialize into any economic benefit, the analysts say. Companies should be able to monetize AI in the future with vertical use cases, like automating procurement or reimagining call centers, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1116 ET - Cracker Barrel abandoned its logo change and restaurant redesign, but other elements of the company's transformation plan will be leaned into more heavily, Benchmark analyst Todd Brooks says in a research note. Those elements have been working and can drive both increased traffic and customer frequency as well as stronger profitability, the analyst says. Management has already teased that it will double down on its menu innovation efforts, which have proven successful for the brand, Brooks says. The company will also continue to work on back-of-house efficiency efforts and drive further impact from the brand's 9 million-member loyalty program, whose members drive more than a third of Cracker Barrel's revenue, the analyst says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: JPMorgan Chase hires two senior bankers to bolster consumer and retail investment team
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase has hired two managing directors to strengthen its consumer and retail investment banking unit, according to an internal memo seen by Reuters on Friday.

Brian Esmond will join in Los Angeles from Centerview Partners. Bryan Fleming will be based in New York after leaving Bank of America , where he worked as a managing director in retail and consumer investment banking.

Both are expected to start later this year and will report to Jonathan Dunlop, Patrick Gallagher and Aaron Weisbrod, the memo said.

JPMorgan's investment banking fees rose 7% to $2.5 billion in the second quarter from a year earlier, underpinned by a rise in mergers and acquisitions and debt underwriting.

The move was first reported by Bloomberg News.

data.title: Apple Watch Adds FDA-Approved Blood Pressure Alerts Using AI
data.description:
Apple (AAPL, Financials) is adding a new health feature to its Watch Series 11 alerts that can warn users when they may have high blood pressure. The tool, powered by artificial intelligence, was cleared by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and will be available in more than 150 countries.

The feature works by analyzing existing heart sensor data through AI models trained on research from more than 100,000 study participants. It doesn't measure blood pressure directly, but it nudges users to check with a traditional cuff and follow up with a doctor if needed. Apple says the alerts will also be available on Watch Series 9 and newer devices.

More than a billion individuals throughout the world have high blood pressure, and many of them don't know it. Experts in health suggest that the alarms on the Apple Watch could help more individuals find out about the ailment early. They also say that the tool shouldn't be used instead of a doctor's diagnosis, and people who don't get an alert shouldn't think they're safe.

The launch for Apple builds on years of work to make the Watch more than just a fitness tracker. It now has blood pressure warnings in addition to heart rhythm tracking and other functions. Both investors and users will be watching to see how many people use the new tool and whether it helps Apple get more people interested in health.

data.title: Elon Musk's Neuralink plans brain implant trial for speech impairments
data.description:
Billionaire Elon Musk’s brain-implant company Neuralink plans to launch a trial in October aimed at helping people with speech impairments translate their thoughts into text, the company’s president said.

Dongjin “D.J.” Seo told an audience at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology in Seoul this week that the study targets people who have lost the ability to speak, enabling them "to go directly from brain to voice without any keyboards in between."

"If you're imagining saying something, we would be able to pick that up," he said.

Neuralink said the U.S. Food and Drug Administration has granted the device a Breakthrough Device designation for speech, a status that can expedite development, assessment and review.

Neuralink did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The company began human trials in 2024 after addressing safety concerns raised by the FDA, which had rejected an earlier application in 2022.

Last month, Neuralink said 12 people worldwide have received its chip, logging more than 15,000 hours of use.

Neuralink’s implant is designed to help people with conditions such as spinal cord injury. The first patient has used it to play video games, browse the internet, post on social media and move a cursor on a laptop.

Rival Synchron Inc is also testing an implant to help people with motor impairment type on a computer.

data.title: Trump says to host Turkey's Erdogan at White House on Sept. 25
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Friday that he will host Turkey's President Tayyip Erdogan at the White House on Sept. 25 and that he expected to conclude trade and military agreements.

"We are working on many Trade and Military Deals with the President, including the large scale purchase of Boeing aircraft, a major F-16 Deal, and a continuation of the F-35 talks, which we expect to conclude positively," he wrote on Truth Social.

Erdogan last visited the White House in 2019 during Trump's first term, and the pair have had a checkered past.

While they had close personal bonds during Trump's 2017-21 term as president, it was also a period of strained bilateral relations due to disputes over Washington's ties with Kurdish fighters in Syria and over Ankara's ties with Moscow.

Turkey angered the Trump administration in 2019 by purchasing Russian S-400 missile defenses. In response, Washington canceled a planned sale of F-35 fighter jets to Turkey and ousted it from a joint production program for the planes.

Turkey subsequently agreed on a deal to purchase F-16 jets.

"President Erdogan and I have always had a very good relationship. I look forward to seeing him on the 25th!" Trump wrote on Friday.

data.title: Major U.S. Stock Indexes Rise; Senate Rejects Spending Bill — WSJ
data.description:
By Caitlin McCabe

President Trump announced a TikTok deal with Chinese leader Xi Jinping after the two spoke today.

"I just completed a very productive call with President Xi of China. We made progress on many very important issues including trade, fentanyl, the need to bring the war between Russia and Ukraine to an end, and the approval of the TikTok deal," he said on his Truth Social platform.

Trump and Xi didn't release details of the deal, but new investors would own about half the company, while existing ones in TikTok parent ByteDance including Susquehanna International, KKR and General Atlantic would hold roughly 30%.

In addition, lawmakers on Capitol Hill voted Friday to reject a short-term spending bill that aimed to avoid a government shutdown at the end of the month. The vote was 44 in favor and 48 against, short of the 60 required and sending GOP leaders back to the drawing board.

Stocks were mostly higher Friday, a day after the Russell 2000 and the three major U.S. stock indexes closed at all-time highs for the first time since November 2021.

The Bank of Japan kept interest rates on hold at the end of a week in which the Federal Reserve and several other central banks cut them. The BoJ said it would start offloading its portfolio of exchange-traded funds, a sign of confidence in the economy despite global trade tumult.

In Friday trading:

  • Treasury yields rose for a third day. The 10-year yield touched 4.14%.
  • Gold futures advanced above $3,680 a troy ounce, hovering near all-time highs.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Apple has asked suppliers to boost production of iPhone 17 model, the Information reports
data.description:
Apple has told at least two suppliers to boost production of the entry-level model iPhone 17 by at least 30% after strong pre-orders last weekend, the Information reported on Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The development suggests more consumers than Apple anticipated chose the cheaper $799 iPhone 17 over the premium Pro models, which start at $1,099 and typically offer superior materials, cameras, processors and displays.

Apple did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

The shift underscores how even Apple, long known for pushing customers toward its highest-margin devices, is facing more price-sensitive demand, raising questions about Apple's ability to sustain growth through premium upgrades.

Analysts have said sales of lower-priced models could weigh on Apple's profit margins, even as they help the company defend market share.

data.title: Stocks poised to end week higher after key central bank decisions
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street stocks advance in choppy trading
  • Fed rate cut keeps investors upbeat
  • European shares finish week slightly lower
  • Gold prices rise, crude oil falls

By Chibuike Oguh and Elizabeth Howcroft

Global stocks rose in choppy trading on Friday and were on track for a weekly gain driven by positive sentiment on Wall Street following key central bank decisions.

The U.S. Federal Reserve cut interest rates by a quarter of a percentage point on Wednesday, the first rate cut since December, while Norway and Canada also cut rates.

On Wall Street, all three indexes were trading higher a day after hitting record highs. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.29% to 46,277, the S&P 500 rose 0.29% to 6,650.93, and the Nasdaq Composite rose 0.45% to 22,571.

European shares finished down 0.16%, and for the week were down 0.13%.

Japan's Nikkei fell 0.57% after the Bank of Japan decided to start selling its holdings of risky assets. MSCI's gauge of stocks across the globe EURONEXT:IACWI rose 0.09% to 980.09, hovering near a record high reached in the previous session, and was poised to add 0.82% for the week.

Investors are betting that central bank rate cuts will boost stocks further.

"The market for the past several weeks has all been focused on and relying on the Fed meeting and the Fed's decision, and there was enough in the decision to leave everyone just slightly disappointed though basically satisfied," said Michael Farr, chief executive of investment advisory firm Farr, Miller & Washington in Washington.

The Fed stopped short of endorsing market expectations for a clear string of rate cuts, emphasizing a meeting-by-meeting, data-dependent approach. The Fed's tone, along with the wide range of views within the central bank, disappointed some investors, who had hoped the stock market would be boosted by a rapid shift to lower rates, analysts said.

"Markets have done exceptionally well and now markets are looking for the next driver or the next bit of news," Farr said. "I think as we probably get into earnings season in October, those reports will be more important than ever because we need to see and the Fed needs to see if tariffs are indeed making their way into bottom-line profits."

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes rose 3.3 basis points to 4.137%. The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield, which typically moves in step with interest rate expectations for the Fed, rose 1.2 basis points to 3.568%.

Following their first call in three months, U.S. President Donald Trump said he and Chinese President Xi Jinping made progress on a TikTok agreement and would meet face-to-face in six weeks in South Korea to discuss trade, illicit drugs and Russia's war in Ukraine.

A stopgap spending bill that would avert an Oct. 1 government shutdown fell short in the U.S. Senate on Friday. The bill had been passed by the House of Representatives.

The U.S. dollar index rose for a third straight session, up 0.28% to 97.61, but was still set to notch a third straight week of losses.

The dollar strengthened 0.35% to 0.795 against the Swiss franc , but was down 0.07% to 147.88 against the Japanese yen .

The euro was down 0.3% against the dollar at $1.1751.

The British pound fell, down 0.58% on the day at $1.34975 .

The Bank of England on Thursday kept rates on hold, but slowed the pace at which it is unloading the government bonds it purchased in previous crises.

European government bond yields rose, with Germany's 10-year yield falling 0.3 basis points at 2.747% <DE10YT=RR>. While shorter-dated bonds have benefited from expectations for rate cuts, longer-dated bond yields have risen on investor concern about government finances.

Oil prices were down, as traders' worries about fuel demand outweighed the boost that oil prices would typically get from a U.S. rate cut.

Brent crude futures were down 1.22% at $66.62 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate futures (CLC1) lost 1.32%, to $62.73.

Gold was up 0.77% at $3,672.02, heading for its fifth straight week of gains.

data.title: BNB Joins the Four-Digit Club – But This Time, It’s Different
data.description:
BNB surged past the $1,000 mark after steadily climbing by 10% over the past week. New data suggest that the latest milestone is more than hype.

In fact, several on-chain metrics point to a structural shift in the asset’s market profile.Organic Liquidity Growth

According to CryptoQuant’s latest analysis, BNB’s uptrend has been supported by healthy liquidity rather than unsustainable hype. The Spot Volume Bubble Map reveals consistently rising volumes without the “overheating” extremes that characterized speculative blow-offs in 2021. This indicates that demand is expanding in a controlled, organic manner.

Similarly, the 90-day Spot Taker Cumulative Volume Delta (CVD) hints at a sustained buy-side dominance throughout 2025, in a stark contrast to past cycles where rallies quickly transitioned into selling pressure. This change indicates that accumulation is broad-based and has drawn strength from a diverse set of market participants.

In derivatives, the Futures Taker CVD (90-day) confirms that buy-side dominance strengthened as BNBtouched$1,000, which means institutional interest rather than retail-driven over-leverage. Interestingly, partnerships such as Franklin Templeton’s and the expansion of Digital Asset Tokenization (DAT) initiatives appear to be anchoring futures activity and have lent credibility to the demand base.

These on-chain signals align with network fundamentals, including rising daily active users, increased decentralized exchange (DEX) activity, and steady stablecoin inflows. Such factors point to real utility rather than being speculative, even as short-term volatility remains inevitable.

“In short, BNB’s move to four digits reflects a structural shift: growing retail adoption, ecosystem utility, and institutional recognition are converging. While short-term corrections are possible, the on-chain picture implies that BNB is positioning itself as more than just an exchange token – it is evolving into a digital reserve asset.”BNB Millionaires

The modest investment of a “diamond hand” BNB holder, who spent less than $1,000 eight years ago to accumulate 999 BNB at a price of around $1 each, has now ballooned into nearly $1 million. Lookonchain said that the investment has delivered a staggering 1,000x return. The wallet has held onto the tokens through years of volatility.

One such early investor is sitting on a fortune worth more than $29 million. Blockchain records show that in 2017, the trader purchased 71,500 BNB for roughly $996,500. Soon after, he sold 42,421 BNB, but decided to hold the remaining balance. That long-term decision has proven extraordinarily profitable.

With BNB recently surpassing the $1,000 milestone, his remaining holdings are now worth around $29.24 million. Despite the massive unrealized gains, on-chain data indicates he has yet to sell a single token from the stash.

data.title: Trade Protectionism Rising in Global Steel Markets — Market Talk
data.description: Nations around the world are considering stepping up trade protection measures on the heels of a unified tariff on all U.S. steel imports, Fitch Ratings says. Several countries are considering increasing domestic production, as well as anti-dumping and safeguard measures in less-protected markets such as India, Turkey and Brazil, according to Fitch. The EU could see the introduction of a "melted and poured" rule, Fitch adds. That rule is a trade measure which determines the origin of steel and metal goods by the location of initial melting and casting. "We expect trade flows to shift, with high-cost producers becoming unable to compete with lower-cost steelmakers," Fitch says, adding this could include changes in U.S. import patterns, with higher-cost steel possibly being diverted from the U.S. into less-protected markets. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

data.title: Unusual Machines Appoints Nathaniel Kennedy as Vice President of Marketing
data.description:
Kennedy to lead multi-brand marketing strategy as Unusual Machines advances domestic production

ORLANDO, FLORIDA / ACCESS Newswire / September 19, 2025 / Unusual Machines (NYSE AMERICAN:UMAC), a leading provider of NDAA-compliant drone components, today announced the appointment of Nathaniel Kennedy as Vice President of Marketing. Kennedy will report to Stacy Wright, Executive Vice President of Revenue.

With over 25 years of experience in e-commerce, consumer, enterprise, and licensing, Kennedy has led digital transformation, forged high-profile partnerships-including a NASCAR collaboration-and scaled businesses into national leaders. At Unusual Machines, he will oversee marketing for Rotor Riot, Fat Shark, and the broader portfolio, in addition to supporting enterprise and defense growth.

"The pace of the drone industry demands marketing leadership with foresight and range-especially as we grow our domestic production. Nate brings that perspective, with broad experience across consumer and enterprise markets-including defense-to help guide how we connect our strategy to customers and scale what comes next," said Stacy Wright, EVP of Revenue at Unusual Machines.

"What drew me to Unusual Machines is the clarity of this moment-where marketing's impact directly shapes how growth takes form. It's rare to be in an environment where strategy and execution align so closely, and I'm eager to add my perspective as we expand in key markets like defense and enterprise," said Nathaniel Kennedy, VP of Marketing at Unusual Machines.

About Unusual Machines

Unusual Machines manufactures and sells drone components and drones across a diversified brand portfolio, which includes Fat Shark, the leader in FPV (first-person view) ultra-low latency video goggles for drone pilots. The Company also retails small, acrobatic FPV drones and equipment directly to consumers through the curated Rotor Riot ecommerce store. With a changing regulatory environment, Unusual Machines seeks to be a dominant Tier-1 parts supplier to the fast-growing multi-billion-dollar U.S. drone industry. According to Fact.MR, the global drone accessories market is currently valued at $17.5 billion and is set to top $115 billion by 2032.

For more information, please visit www.unusualmachines.com.
